title: 9 Cubby Bookshelf
summary: A plywood-backed 1x12 cubby shelf — two dividers form nine even openings, with 1x4 shelf trim across every front edge and a full plywood back tying the whole case rigid.
description:
This is a straightforward 1x12 box divided by two full-height uprights into nine cubbies, three across and three tall — a simple grid rather than a face-framed cabinet, so the whole build is pocket holes and a plywood back rather than mitered trim.

Build the outer frame (top, bottom, sides) first with pocket holes placed on the insides and undersides so none of them show, then add the 1x2 bottom trim before the shelves go in — trim now is much easier to nail flush than trim added after the case is full of shelves. The six shelves attach to both the sides and the two dividers with the same pocket-hole approach, and the 1x4 trim across every shelf front comes after, brad-nailed and glued for a finished edge that hides the plywood-adjacent 1x12 edge grain.

The plywood back is what makes this simple grid rigid rather than racking — it's glued and nailed not just around the perimeter but directly into every divider and shelf it crosses, which is what keeps nine open cubbies from shifting into parallelograms under a shelf full of books.

materials.name: 1/4" plywood or hardboard, full sheet (4' x 8')

cutList.material: 1/4" plywood or hardboard, full sheet (4' x 8')

steps.title: Cut the case parts
steps.body: From the 10 ft 1x12s, cut the two sides 59 1/4" long and the two dividers 56"; from the 8 ft 1x12s, cut the top 48", the bottom 46 1/2", and six shelves 15". All are 11 1/4" deep. The widths interlock exactly: the 46 1/2" bottom fits between the two 3/4" sides to make a 48" case, and taking the two 3/4" dividers out of that 46 1/2" leaves 45", which is three 15" cubbies across. From the 1x2 cut two bottom trims 46 1/2"; from the 1x4 cut four shelf trims 48", noting that two 48" pieces use a whole 8 ft board with nothing left for the saw kerf, so cut those carefully. Cut the 1/4" back 48" x 59" from the sheet last, against the case you actually build.
materials: 1/4" plywood or hardboard, full sheet (4' x 8')

steps.title: Build the outer frame
steps.body: Stand the two 59 1/4" sides on edge, fit the 46 1/2" bottom between them, and cap them with the 48" top so its ends are flush with the sides' outside faces. Drill 3/4"-setting pocket holes on the faces that end up inside the case and on the undersides, so not one of them shows on the finished piece; that placement is decided now and cannot be changed later. Join with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue, then measure both diagonals and adjust until they match before moving on.

steps.title: Add the bottom trim
steps.body: Fit the two 46 1/2" 1x2 trims across the bottom of the case, one at the front and one at the back, between the sides. Nail them with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue, keeping the top edge flush with the top face of the bottom board so nothing catches on what slides in and out of the lower cubbies. Do this before the shelves and dividers go in - reaching into a case already full of cubbies to nail a trim flush is far harder than doing it now, which is exactly why the order matters.

steps.title: Add the shelves and dividers
steps.body: Stand the two 56" dividers inside the case, spaced so the three bays come out at 15" each, then fit the six 15" shelves between the sides and the dividers in two rows, dividing the height into three tiers. Work from one side across, pocket-holing each shelf into the side or divider it meets with 1-1/4" screws and glue, and check every shelf level and every divider plumb as you go - nine cubbies means nine chances for a small cumulative error to show. Put the pocket holes on the undersides of the shelves so nobody looking into a cubby sees one.

steps.title: Trim the shelf fronts
steps.body: Run the 48" 1x4 trims across the front of the case at each shelf row, top edge flush with the shelf's top surface, so the trim covers the 1x12's front edge grain and reads as a much thicker shelf. Nail with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue into the front edges of the shelves, the sides, and both dividers. The cut list gives four trims for two shelf rows, so dry-fit all four against the case front and settle where the second pair goes before nailing.

steps.title: Add the back and finish
steps.body: Square the case by measuring the diagonals across the front and adjusting until they match, then lay the 48" x 59" plywood back on and glue and nail it with 3/4" brad nails into every part it crosses: the perimeter, both dividers, and all six shelves. That is what turns nine open squares into a rigid grid rather than nine parallelograms waiting to lean under a shelf of books. Fill the nail holes, sand every surface and every cubby edge, and finish, coating the insides of the cubbies too since those are the faces you actually look at.
materials: 1/4" plywood or hardboard, full sheet (4' x 8')

images.alt: 9-cubby bookshelf with a plywood back and trimmed shelf edges
